speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. The survivor's name is Jerry-- Gerald Steinberg. The interviewer is Donna [? Kenny. ?] We are doing this in Long Meadow, Mass., USA, and the language is English. Continue, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry, you were just explaining what happened after the barn burned at the first barn you'd stayed in for a long period of time-- for eight months, approximately. And you said that your father then went out and contacted other people and found another Gentile farmer to help you and your mother and aunt hide. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you continue with that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second farmer's name was Lishevitc. I believe it's spelled L-I-S-H-E-W-I-C. I'm not too sure about the spelling. But that kind of the way it sounds, I would spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the second barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second farmer was, again, in the same general area. You have to realize that, most of the time, I mean, the whole time my father-- the way he moved around was walking. And this was in the same area of Mlynow and Morawica, little farms that were situated in the countryside. And so it was in the same general area. We never really went very far from maybe within maybe five or 10 kilometers of that whole area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And explain again why you and your family were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were hiding from the Germans because they would kill us if they found us, as they were annihilating all the other. By that time, most of the residents from the ghetto-- from Mlynow and Morawica, all the Jewish residents-- were all killed by that time. And the few that were in hiding, some were discovered and killed. And, of course, later on, as we found out, a few survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe again the environment-- the physical environment that you and your mother and father and aunt were actually in at the second farmer's barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the second farmer's, my father persuaded him, again, to dig a hole in the barn where the cows were kept. And they covered it up with planks. And, again, the entrance was through the manger. And, again, the hole was very small, just big enough to hold four people in a lying position. It certainly wasn't tall enough to stand up. And-- and certainly, movement was very limited inside, to a point where, you know, if one person turned, the other person would have to turn at the same time to be able to move. Again, the conditions were, of course, very damp, as the cows were on the top and just the planks. And things used to go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what you mean by 'things used to go through'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the cows, of course, would urinate and whatever. And it would go through, so the hole was very damp. And the only thing that we had to line the hole was straw. And what little clothes we had was very minimal. They were just like rags, and certainly, our feet and most of our body was bare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no blankets or-- most of the things that we had were like rags and from potato sacks. They-- they [? would. ?] Whatever we had available, they would just wrap our feet and-- and body with sacks and so forth. But there were-- the clothes were just tattered and worn out. And, of course, they would get wet and so forth. So was not very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being cold or hot?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was very cold most of the time. And, again, I remember the dampness and so forth, and the darkness. I personally remember that, and so on. I don't remember too many of the other specifics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any of the three adults leave the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Occasionally, my father would go out during the night and see if he could find some extra food for us. The-- the people and the farmers were, themselves, quite poor, and they had just about enough food to feed themselves. And as kind as they would try to be to give us a little bit, I mean, good food would be a potato or a piece of bread. And that would be the-- the-- the good food to have. But occasionally, my father would go out and-- and pick berries or-- or fruit when it was available. He would literally steal it from other farmers and so forth, in the woods, and-- and bring it back so we'd-- we'd stay alive. I'm told by the time that we were liberated, we were really just skin and bones and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned eating a potato, that that would be a significant event.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be a very special thing to eat, if there was any available. And occasionally, my father would even go through the-- the food that they would give to the pigs, and that would be some leftover things, possibly spoiled things. And he would find something that he felt was edible, and he would bring it back into the hole, and we would use that to stay alive as food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that one reason the hole was so damp was because of the cows urinating through the floor planks above you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did all four of you do in terms of relieving yourselves, and being able to go to the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really don't recall. But you'll probably-- [INAUDIBLE] I am sure I probably urinated right there and so forth. It would be absorbed into the ground. But the sanitary conditions were almost nonexistent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you all do in terms of satiating your thirst? Did you have water available to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would have water. Of course, they would leave water for the cows. So occasionally, my father would find a way to bring water down to us. And again, it was probably very minimal, just enough to stay alive and comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this barn also, were you able to go up to the top occasionally and see daylight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same way, right, just on occasion. At one point, I would-- I'm told I was going up to the attic of the barn. And just as I was going up on a ladder, the door opened up to the barn and we were almost discovered. And fortunately, that did not occur. But then as a result of that, my going up to the attic became more infrequent.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the door to the barn opened, and you were almost discovered. Who entered the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people. I really don't know specifically. I don't think it was the Germans. It was probably perhaps other farmers or neighbors that came with the farmer, and they-- for whatever reason, they had to go into the barn and so forth. But I-- on many occasions, my parents would mention that to us after we were liberated, that we were quite fortunate that, at that point in time, we were not discovered because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at this barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: This second farmer, again, kept us for approximately maybe eight or nine months, again. And this time, he became very nervous about-- because he would hear that other farmers were caught hiding Jews. And of course, they would be killed, along with the Jews. So he became quite nervous. And as a result, he made us leave. And, again, my father persuaded him to keep us a couple extra days while he, again, went out. And, again, every time my father would go out would be during the night. And, again, he went out contacting other acquaintances, other farmers in the area, that he had confidence in their friendship. And, again, he persuaded another farmer to hide us in a similar fashion as the first two. And so my father came back and took us and brought us to the third farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately what time of year and what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the fall. It seems every time we had to move around, it-- it was getting cold, and it was cold and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember feeling cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And, again, my father would kind of persuade the farmer-- that was part-- part of the persuasion process-- that it was very cold. And-- and he would have pity on us, so to speak, and take us in because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was 1944, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think that brings us into 1944 and so on, during the last, the third farmer, we were liberated kind of the end of '44, beginning of '45, during that winter. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of this third Gentile farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: His last name was Obanuk, O-B-A-N-U-K. Again, the spelling might not be exactly. But this is the way, again, the name sounds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there a shorter period of time, maybe five or six months. The total hiding time was a little over two years, between all the farmers that we-- that hid us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then to make you leave this farmer's barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last farmer, of course, was the time that we're liberated, and the word was getting out that the Germans were already being-- losing, and they were moving out of the area. And eventually, the-- the Russians came and went over-- you know, kind of kicked the Germans out of that area. And when my father heard that, he-- he went out. And he took my mother and myself and my aunt out of the hole. And, of course, we looked like almost dead people. And the Russian soldiers almost couldn't believe what they saw, and took pity on us. And this is when we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father know that the Russians were liberating the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he would hear from the farmer. And, again, occasionally he would go out into the woods at night, and he would meet up with other people. And the word was out that the Russians were close. And, again, he had a fear because sometimes the Russians would come in, and the Germans would kind of rearm and kick them back out. And they would retreat and then come back in again. But fortunately for us, that that did not happen. But I'm told I was one of the concerns that my father had at the time before he actually took us out of hiding and went in with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day you were liberated with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The actual day, no. But I do remember, of course, us going back to the farm and the way the farm looked at the time, and so forth, that we lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to visit the last farm where you had been hiding? Is that what you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not where we were hiding, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you had lived originally--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We went.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Prior to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that farm looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain things, I remember very vividly, and-- and certain areas [? way ?] I-- where I played with my cousin. And I remember where the horses stood. And we used to jump off the roof of that little shed where they were kept. And I remember specifically that we had some pigs. This-- this is later on, maybe a few weeks later, the pigs-- one of the pigs gave birth. And I remember there was a whole big to-do about that and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left the third farmer's barn, did you immediately go back to your original family farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. My father, of course, kind of scouted around the area with the Russians and, with their help, was able to go back to the-- to the farm. [PAUSES 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at the family farm when you did return after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- We-- we didn't stay very long, perhaps several months. And then my father, I guess through conversations with others, found that it was not very safe for us to-- to stay in that area. And he was able to procure papers as us being Polish citizens, to move further into Poland. And so we packed up, and-- and-- so to speak, and moved to another part of Poland. That part of Poland became part of Russia, of course. So that-- he felt that would be safer. And, of course, my father had visions of eventually emigrating to Israel, which was then Palestine. So that was the original intent. But as Polish citizens, we were able to move to another part of Poland, to a city called Bytom. I believe it's spelled B-Y-T-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately how far from Mlynow is Bytom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. Maybe several hundred kilometers. But it was a large city. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- we were just there a short period of time. And, again, my father was able to procure additional papers, because you couldn't move from one part of the country to another or from one country to another unless you had certain papers. And my father was able to get additional papers to enable us to go to Germany to the American zone, again, with the intent of emigrating to Israel. And that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first time that I actually had seen a railroad, and I remember going on a railroad car. And with other-- we met up with other refugees at that time, and we began traveling towards Germany. And, again, we were stopped in Czechoslovakia. They stopped the train with all the refugees on it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was the Czech government. And I also believe that, I think, the United States had something to do with that territory at that time, just from what I recall-- the conversations of my father and other people afterwards. And apparently, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever papers that we had were not-- they said they were not valid. And they made everybody get off the train, and they put us into a large-- I believe it was like a large hall or a gymnasium. And they detained everybody there for several months while they were going through the legal hassle of allowing us to continue on our journey. And fortunately, again, they straightened everything out. And again, I believe there was certain intervention with the American government at that time. And they allowed us to continue on to the American part of German-- occupied Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when would this have been happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- would be pretty close to, like, 1945. I'd say the middle of 1945, middle to the end of 1945. And from Czechoslovakia, again, we went into Germany. And they placed us into a DP camp, a displaced persons camp, under the auspices of the American government.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you a couple of questions about what you've been describing in terms of that transition period after the liberation by the Russians and en route to the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said that the train was stopped and you all were detained for a couple of months, where did you live during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They first put us in this large hall, and they would just more or less give each family group a little cubicle within the hall. And eventually, my father-- I kind of remember this-- my mother found some blankets and things to put on the floor to make us more comfortable, as well as finding, I believe, what, like, used doors that would kind of surround the little area that we were at. That part, I remember. I believe after that, they put us into, like, an old hotel or something for a short period of time. I don't remember that too much, but I do remember the large hall and the way we were situated on the floor of the hall and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were involved in that detainment?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were several hundred people, I believe, in that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the governments involved feeding you all?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was food, I-- I remember. I also remember going out. This was a very special time, I guess, going out into the streets. And at that time, we already had some clothing on us. And there were little huts, and they used to sell little small rolls with meat inside-- little sandwiches. I remember having those, and that was a very special treat. Those are little things I remember. And that was in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a pet or an animal you became attached to while you were a little boy?

words.text: while you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right after the war, my father had gotten me a dog, which stayed with us through the time that we were in Bytom. He was unable to continue on beyond that point. And I was very fond of this dog. His name was Rex. And I still remember that. He was a German shepherd type of dog. And I remember, I've always liked dogs. And having him at that time was very comforting for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had procured Rex specifically for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long did you have Rex in your life at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably close to a year, from the time after we were liberated to the time that we went through Bytom-- our stay at Bytom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what became of Rex?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they just got rid of him or gave him away to somebody. I really don't remember, specifically, what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue talking about your life after the liberation, is there anything that you would like to add at this point regarding your time in hiding as a young child? Is there anything we didn't talk about concerning that period of your life that you'd like to elaborate upon or revisit in our discussion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think, you know, in retrospect, certainly we were very, very lucky. And it was-- I guess, the only way to describe it is it's like a miracle, certain events happening and not happening, that meant us being survivors and us being killed with the others. It was just pure-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] just pure luck or-- or just a special kind of sense that my father had-- instinct, if you will-- to do certain things, or certain things happening, that kept us alive. And I think it's just the only way to describe it is like a miracle. Certain things that I-- my father and my parents, over the years, have talked about, and certain things that would happen at certain times. They were pure coincidences that meant us surviving and not surviving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does an example come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Recently, my father at this time always observes the time of death-- it's called Yahrzeit-- for those that got killed. And specifically, within this last couple of weeks, we observed the Yahrzeit of my mother's side of the family that managed to escape from the ghetto, and they were also hiding out on a farm of the Gentiles. And at one time-- this was even before our first place of hiding. It was just a transition [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place where we hid out in a barn in the straw. And my father and mother had gone out to procure to see that-- when they went into the ghetto, they gave certain valuables to various farmers in the area that they trusted. And my father would periodically go out and take some things to give as payment, in a sense, or kind of show his appreciation to the farmers that hid us. And this one evening, my mother and my father left me and my aunt hiding. And they went out to one of the farmers to get it. And a lot of times, I'm told my mother would just hide in the-- outside the barn somewhere, and my father would go in the house and so on. And on the way back, they-- my father was going to go visit. He knew-- somehow, he found out where my mother's brothers were hiding, and he was going to go visit them. And my mother said-- she was kind of nervous about it. She said, gee, we left, you know, me with my aunt. And she said, well, let's go visit them some other time. And then they-- they did not go to visit the farm where my mother's brothers were hidden. And that was the time that they were discovered and killed by the Germans. So through some fate, my parents did not go visit the family, and-- or they would have been killed right at that same time. So these are little things that kind of, for whatever reason they happened, miraculously enabled us to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother learn, then, about the fate of her brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortly after that, again, through the conversations with the farmer that was hiding us. And during the times my father would go out into the woods and meet up with other people hiding, that they would find out about various people and so on. Another incident that comes to mind was that, during one of these forages into the woods that my father went out, he met up with the parents of a young lady who kind of comes into our lives after being liberated. They-- they had told him that they had given their daughter, who was approximately the same age as I was-- about eight months younger, actually-- they had given her away to a Polish family. And shortly thereafter, my father found out that the parents were killed. And after we were liberated, my father remembered this incident. And he went and took the girl back, with a great deal of resistance from the Polish family. And my father, I'm told, had to get the-- the Russian soldiers to come with him to help him take her back from the Polish family. And later on, she-- she was with us right through the period from the time that the Russians liberated us through the time that we spent in a DP camp in Germany. And they actually went and adopted her during the time that we were in a DP camp, as-- as their child, or my sister. And unfortunately-- or fortunately, whichever way you look at it-- she had a-- an aunt in Israel-- Palestine at that time. And somehow, my parents corresponded with the aunt. And the aunt found out that we had her, and she insisted that she come live with her in Israel. And although, legally, my parents could have kept her, and my mother was a very religious person and a righteous person, and she felt that perhaps it would be best that she go to live with her aunt. Her aunt kind of appealed to her religious fair being, I guess, if you will.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your adopted sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Aviva, and first name is still Aviva, A-V-I-V-A. That's her first name. And her maiden name was Feldman, F-E-L-D-M-A-N. And she has a family now. She lives in Israel, and she has her own family. And her married name is-- last name is Morami, M-O-R-A-M-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This concludes the second tape. And we'll continue in just a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
